title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted file uploads are serious, especially if they're affecting multiple customers.

To help me scope this out: **when did this start happening?** Was there a recent deploy, configuration change, or update to TaskFlow before you first noticed the corruption? And can you confirm — are PDFs that were uploaded *before* this started still opening fine, or are those broken too?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It started maybe a week ago? I'm not totally sure of the exact date. Old PDFs that were uploaded before seem fine, it's just the new ones. Look, I don't know what you guys changed on your end but something broke. Can you just fix it? Our customers are complaining and we look bad.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
I understand the urgency — corrupted attachments visible to your customers is a big deal, and we want to get this sorted quickly.

A couple of specifics that will help us zero in on the problem fast:

**Is it only PDFs that are affected, or have you seen corruption with other file types too** (e.g., images, Word docs, spreadsheets)?

Also — if you re-download a corrupted PDF and compare its file size to the original on your machine, are they roughly the same size, or is the uploaded version noticeably smaller or larger? This tells us whether the file is being truncated or mangled during transfer.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Honestly I mostly deal with PDFs so that's what I've noticed. I haven't really checked other file types carefully. I just know our PDFs are broken and customers are upset. I didn't compare file sizes, I just know they won't open. Actually now that I think about it, I feel like the smaller PDFs we send might be okay? But I'm not sure, I haven't tested it systematically or anything. Can you just look at what changed on your end recently? Something clearly broke.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That detail about smaller PDFs possibly working is really helpful — it points us toward a specific class of bug.

Could you do a quick test for me? **Try uploading a very small PDF (under 1 MB) and a larger one (say 5+ MB) and let me know which one comes through intact and which one is corrupted.** If you can tell me the approximate file sizes of both, even better.

Also, when you try to open a corrupted PDF, **what exactly does the error say?** For example, does your PDF viewer say "file is damaged," "unexpected end of file," "not a valid PDF," or something else? The specific wording helps us distinguish between truncation (file cut short) vs. encoding corruption (file mangled).

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Look, I'm not a QA tester, I'm trying to manage my customers here. But fine — I just tried it. I uploaded a little one-page PDF, maybe a couple hundred KB, and that one opened fine. Then I uploaded a bigger report, like 3 or 4 MB, and that one's broken. The error just says the file is damaged or corrupted, I don't remember the exact wording. So yeah, small ones seem okay, big ones are busted. Now can someone please look at whatever you changed a week ago? Something obviously broke.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Large file uploads (>~1MB) are silently corrupted — size-dependent, regression from ~1 week ago","problem":"PDF files uploaded to task attachments are corrupted and cannot be opened. The corruption is size-dependent: small files (a few hundred KB) upload successfully, while larger files (3-4MB+) are damaged. This began approximately one week ago and affects multiple customers. Previously uploaded files remain intact.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A recent change (~1 week ago) to the file upload pipeline is truncating or mangling files above a size threshold. Most likely candidates: (1) a request body size limit was reduced (e.g., nginx/reverse proxy max_body_size, API gateway limit), (2) chunked transfer encoding was introduced or broken, (3) a streaming/buffering change is cutting off uploads mid-transfer, or (4) a base64 or multipart encoding change is corrupting the byte stream for payloads above a certain size.","reproduction_steps":["Upload a small PDF (<500KB) to a task attachment — confirm it opens correctly","Upload a larger PDF (3-5MB) to a task attachment","Download the larger PDF and attempt to open it — expect 'file is damaged' error","Compare the byte size of the original and uploaded file to determine if truncation occurred","Check hex dump of the corrupted file: does it end abruptly (truncation) or contain garbled bytes (encoding corruption)?"],"environment":"Production environment, started approximately one week ago. Specific browser/OS not confirmed but reported across multiple customers, suggesting server-side issue.","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple customers are affected. File attachments are a core workflow feature and corrupted files are visible to end-users, causing customer-facing reputation damage. Small files still work, providing a partial (but impractical) workaround.","recommended_fix":"1. Review all deployments and config changes from the past 7-10 days that touch file upload, storage, or proxy configuration. 2. Check reverse proxy / API gateway settings for request body size limits (e.g., nginx client_max_body_size, AWS API Gateway payload limit). 3. Check for changes to multipart parsing, streaming, or chunked transfer encoding in the upload handler. 4. Upload a known-good 5MB PDF in a staging environment and compare the stored bytes against the original (diff at byte level) to determine whether this is truncation or encoding corruption. 5. If truncation, the file size delta will reveal the exact cutoff point, which likely maps to a config value.","proposed_test_case":"Upload PDFs of varying sizes (100KB, 1MB, 2MB, 5MB, 10MB) and verify that each downloaded file is byte-identical to the original. This should be an automated integration test against the upload endpoint that asserts Content-Length and SHA-256 hash match between source and stored file.","information_gaps":["Exact error message from the PDF viewer (truncation vs. encoding clue — but developer can determine this from byte comparison)","Whether non-PDF file types are also affected (reporter only uses PDFs regularly)","Exact date the issue started and corresponding deployment","Whether the corruption manifests differently across browsers or upload methods"]}
